text: The surgeon holds the liver aside while dissecting the stomach and moves towards the chest. The diaphragm is visible in the upper part of the field, and the gullet is being grasped and cut, clearing surrounding tissue.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to separate tissues around the esophagus, with a fan retractor holding back the liver. This dissection frees the esophagus, which extends from the chest to the abdomen.
The surgeon identifies the pulsating aorta beneath the surgical field, visible as a large artery extending from the heart down into the chest cavity.

The surgeon continues the dissection into the mediastinum, the central compartment of the thoracic cavity, where the esophagus is located. The esophagus, also known as the gullet or food pipe, is being carefully separated from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon continues the dissection to free the esophagus from surrounding tissues, carefully moving the diaphragmatic surface of the heart away from the esophagus. The pulsatile movement of the heart is visible as the dissection progresses to maximize the extent of esophageal liberation.
The surgeon exposes the entire esophagus, or gullet, as it extends into the surgical field. The dissection continues from the neck to remove the remaining portion of the esophagus.

The laparoscopic view shows the mobilized esophagus being pulled out from the neck, along with a portion of the stomach. The newly created artificial tube from the stomach is stitched to the remaining esophagus above, completing the thoracoscopic esophagectomy.
